Intro1: Most people are overconfident in their judgements about the world, which can lead to reckless or unwise decision-making. This problem may worsen with AI use due to its ability to produce convincing but superficial arguments that sweep ambiguity under the rug. When easy answers are provided automatically, we may find ourselves accepting bad ideas due to an inability to recognise false certainty.
Intro2: The goal of these exercises is to calibrate confidence. Being well calibrated means correctly identifying situations when you are likely to be right, and having appropriate uncertainty when things are unclear. This skill plays a central role in learning, reasoning, and decision-making, helping people avoid both systematic overconfidence as well as unwarranted doubt (Lichtenstein, Fischhoff, & Phillips, 1982; Griffin & Tversky, 1992).
Practice1: These problems are about making uncertainty explicit and forcing you to think about the limits of your knowledge. The goal here isn’t to know the answer, it is to reflect upon how sure you are that your answer is correct. Importantly, different people will have different levels of confidence depending on their expertise. For example, a HR manager should be highly confident about employment law, but much less sure about technical issues related to engineering or biology. This means that the “correct” answer(s) are the one that reflects your personal level of knowledge, and different answers will be better for different people.
Practice2: Typical examples involve estimating how many statements are true or judging the likelihood that an answer is correct. Even brief exposure to this kind of feedback can improve awareness of confidence errors and promote more accurate self-assessment over time (Koriat, Lichtenstein, & Fischhoff, 1980; Moore & Healy, 2008).
Ref_Lichtenstein: Lichtenstein, S., Fischhoff, B., & Phillips, L. D. (1982). Calibration of probabilities: The state of the art to 1980. In Judgment under uncertainty: Heuristics and biases (pp. 306–334). Cambridge University Press.
Ref_Griffin: Griffin, D., & Tversky, A. (1992). The weighing of evidence and the determinants of confidence. Cognitive Psychology, 24(3), 411–435.
Ref_Koriat: Koriat, A., Lichtenstein, S., & Fischhoff, B. (1980). Reasons for confidence. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Learning and Memory, 6(2), 107–118.
Ref_Moore: Moore, D. A., & Healy, P. J. (2008). The trouble with overconfidence. Psychological Review, 115(2), 502–517.
